title: Pakistani Chapli Kebab

ingredients.name: minced beef (with some fat, not lean)
tomato, seeds removed and finely chopped

subs.name: 1 tbsp besan (gram flour) mixed with 2 tbsp water
subs.notes: egg-free binder; texture is slightly less crisp

subs.name: 2 tbsp rice flour
subs.notes: makes the crust crispier and is gluten-free

steps.text: Squeeze all excess moisture from the chopped onion and seed the tomato before chopping — excess liquid prevents a crisp crust. Combine the mince, onion, tomato, green chillies, coriander leaves, and mint in a large bowl.

Add the egg, wheat flour, all the dry spices, and salt. Knead the mixture firmly with your hands for 3 minutes until everything is evenly combined and the mince holds together when pressed. Unlike seekh kebab, you want to see some texture — do not over-process.
Divide into 8 portions. Press each between your palms to form a flat, round patty about 1 cm thick and 10 cm across — thinner than an Indian kebab. Work the edges to prevent cracking.

Heat oil in a wide frying pan on medium-high heat. The oil should be 3–4 mm deep. Test with a small piece of mince — if it sizzles immediately, the oil is ready. Add 3–4 kebabs at a time without crowding.

Cook for 4–5 minutes on the first side without touching, until the underside is dark brown and crisp. Flip carefully and cook the second side for 3–4 minutes. The centre should feel firm and the edges should look dry. Drain on kitchen paper.
Serve hot with sliced raw onion, a tomato slice, a squeeze of lemon, and naan or paratha. These are best eaten immediately while the crust is crisp.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → 500 g lamb mince (lamb works equally well); egg → 1 tbsp besan (gram flour) mixed with 2 tbsp water (egg-free binder; texture is slightly less crisp); wheat-flour → 2 tbsp rice flour (makes the crust crispier and is gluten-free)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A regular Indian tawa or cast-iron frying pan works perfectly — use enough oil to coat the base generously, 4–5 mm deep, for the authentic crispy edge. Minced beef is available in Muslim butcher shops across India; substitute with mutton mince from any Indian market. The spices are all standard Indian pantry staples. Chapli kebab pairs wonderfully with Indian roomali roti or laccha paratha.
